dominant_error_pattern: Students assume unfamiliarity with an assessment tool requires permanent avoidance, missing the ethical permission for supervised skill-building and competence development.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A forbids using any unfamiliar tool. Correct answer B allows supervised practice before use. Teach: competence is buildable, not static. NASP permits use of unfamiliar tools if you (1) review the manual, (2) practice administration, (3) get expert supervision on your administration, (4) document the training. New tools + appropriate scaffolding = ethical path forward. Permanent refusal is actually avoidant.

dominant_error_pattern: Students select distractor numbers by proximity to realistic supervision capacity or by confusing NASP recommendations with common practice, missing the explicit ratio standard.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors B (three), C (four), D (eight) all sound plausible but exceed NASP guidance. Teach: NASP explicitly recommends no more than TWO interns per supervisor at once. This is not aspirational—it's the standard for adequate supervision. Common practice in understaffed districts often violates this. Know the standard, advocate to it, and document when you're assigned more.

dominant_error_pattern: Students underestimate or overestimate intern supervision capacity, picking extreme responses (one only) or higher numbers, instead of recalling the standard NASP ratio.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (one), C (three), D (four) each represent different thresholds. NASP standard is two. Teach: one is overcautious and wastes supervisor time; three or four is understaffed and harms supervisee learning. Two allows mentoring attention and peer-learning dynamics. This is both an ethics question and a practical one—two is the sweet spot for growth.

dominant_error_pattern: Students misunderstand NASP's jurisdiction, assuming it can revoke licenses (state board power) or mandate personal actions, rather than recognizing its limits to membership discipline.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor D claims NASP can revoke licenses (false—only state boards can). Distractors A-C are actual NASP powers (expulsion, training requirements, apology demands). Teach: NASP is a professional association, not a licensing authority. When NASP sanctions a member: they may expel, require training, recommend remediation. They cannot revoke your license—that's the state board's power. Clarify jurisdictional boundaries.

dominant_error_pattern: Students confuse the NASP ethics code or certification guidelines with the broad statutory framework that undergirds support for all students, missing ESSA's universal scope.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (ethics code), B (IDEA), C (certification guidelines) are all real documents but serve narrower purposes. ESSA (Every Student Succeeds Act) is the federal framework defining how schools support all students, including general education. IDEA covers only students with disabilities. NASP ethics guide practice; ESSA guides policy. Teach: ESSA is foundational to ALL students, not a subset.
